title: Lesson 34 — The Critic

words.definition: bitter and abusive in language or tone
words.example: A vituperative review accused the author of outright fraud.
The scandal brought public opprobrium down on the firm.
The debate collapsed into personal invective.

words.definition: a false and malicious statement damaging someone's reputation
words.example: He sued the paper over what he called pure calumny.

words.definition: an attack on someone's reputation or integrity
words.example: She refused to cast aspersions on a rival's character.

words.definition: damaging the reputation of someone through false statements
words.example: The magazine retracted the defamatory claim within a week.
The critic's scathing pan closed the show in a week.
Reviews were tepid, neither praising nor condemning.
A laudable effort, even if the results fell short.
Her commendable patience never once wavered.

words.definition: serving as a desirable model; outstandingly good
words.example: The firefighters showed exemplary courage that night.
He received a medal for meritorious service.

story: The critic had two settings and nothing in between. When she hated a book, her review turned [[vituperative|vituperative]], heaping [[opprobrium|opprobrium]] on the author in long paragraphs of [[invective|invective]] that bordered on [[calumny|calumny]]; colleagues sometimes accused her of casting [[aspersion|aspersions]] so wild they were nearly [[defamatory|defamatory]]. A merely disappointing book earned only a [[scathing|scathing]] paragraph; a forgettable one, a [[tepid|tepid]] shrug. But when a book impressed her, she praised its [[laudable|laudable]], [[commendable|commendable]] ambition, called the prose [[exemplary|exemplary]], and judged the whole effort thoroughly [[meritorious|meritorious]] — the rare review authors actually hoped to receive.
